project.title: Non-coding autoimmune risk variant defines role for ICOS in T peripheral helper cell development
project.projectDescription: Our fine-mapping of 76 non-MHC loci associated with risk for rheumatoid arthritis (RA, 11,475 cases, 15,870 controls) has recently identified rs117701653, a non-coding single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) in the CTLA4/CD28/ICOS locus, as the variant most likely to modulate RA risk within this region, with the minor allele (C) showing disease protection compared to the major allele (A). Notably, this SNP exhibits allele-specific protein binding, further supporting its regulatory nature, including greater binding of proteins from Jurkat T cell nuclear extract to the protective allele (C) than to the risk allele (A) by electrophoretic mobility shift assay (EMSA). To identify this protein or protein complex, we applied an efficient DNA pulldown technique, flanking restriction enhanced pulldown (FREP), using nuclear extract from Jurkat T cells, bait DNA corresponding to the (C) allele of rs117701653, competitor DNA fragment, and irrelevant DNA sequence as a negative control. Mass spectrometry analysis of peptides released from FREP identified 43 proteins. Our strongest candidate was structural maintenance of chromosomes flexible hinge domain-containing protein 1 (SMCHD1), which exhibited specific binding to the (C) allele of rs117701653. We confirmed the allelic affinity of SMCHD1 using multiple orthogonal approaches, including FREP and western blotting, EMSA with anti-SMCHD1 antibodies, and CHIP-qPCR with CRISPR-modified Jurkat clones bearing different genotype at rs117701653. In this study, we identified SMCHD1, a chromatin regulator that binds allelically to the rs117701653 allele (C) associated with protection against RA risk.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Beads were conditioned by washing them at least five times with 100 µL of 50 mM ammonium bicarbonate. Subsequently, Trypsin/LysC mix was added to the beads, and the samples were incubated at 37°C overnight to ensure complete digestion. Following digestion, the samples were either centrifuged or placed on a magnetic plate (if magnetic beads were used) to remove the supernatant. The peptide mixtures were then dried using a speed-vac for approximately 1 hour. The dried samples were reconstituted in 50 µL of HPLC solvent A (0.1% formic acid in water), and desalted using C18 ZipTips as described in PMID: 22470061. On the day of analysis, the samples were re-suspended in 10 µL of HPLC solvent A. A nano-scale reverse-phase HPLC capillary column was prepared by packing 3 µm C18 spherical silica beads into a 10 cm long fused silica capillary with a 75 µm internal diameter. The column was equipped with a flame-drawn tip for improved sample introduction efficiency. After equilibrating the column with HPLC solvent A, each sample was loaded using an auto sampler onto the column. A linear gradient of 1% to 38% acetonitrile in B buffer (100% acetonitrile) was applied over 60 minutes to elute the peptides. The elution process was carefully controlled and monitored. As peptides were eluted, they were ionized using electrospray ionization (ESI) and then introduced into a Thermo QExactive HF Orbitrap mass spectrometer for analysis. The mass spectrometer was operated in a data-dependent acquisition mode to ensure comprehensive analysis of the peptide mixtures.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Peptides were detected, isolated, and fragmented to generate tandem mass spectra using the specific fragment ions for each peptide. The resulting peak lists were produced by MSConvert. Peptide sequences and thus protein identities were determined by matching the acquired fragmentation patterns against the UniProt Human 20170412 database, which contains 21,042 sequences and 11,504,947 residues, using the Mascot 2.6 search algorithm (PMID: 18216375). The search included a reversed version of all sequences in the database, and the resulting data was filtered to achieve a peptide false discovery rate between one and two percent.
